questions.q: Walk me through the EF Core migration workflow from model change to database update.

answer.oneLiner: Change the C# model, run `dotnet ef migrations add` to generate Up/Down code, then run `dotnet ef database update` to apply the change to the database.
answer.detail: When you run `dotnet ef migrations add <Name>`, the tooling diffs your current DbContext model against the snapshot file from the last migration. It generates a new migration class with two methods: Up() applies the change and Down() reverses it. The snapshot file is also updated to reflect the new model state. When you run `dotnet ef database update`, EF compares pending migration names against the rows in the `__EFMigrationsHistory` table and executes only the ones not yet applied. Each applied migration name is inserted into that table so it is not re-run. At runtime, `ctx.Database.MigrateAsync()` does the same thing programmatically, but should only be used in development or single-instance setups because multiple pods racing to migrate can cause failures.
keyPoints: `migrations add` diffs the model against the snapshot and generates Up/Down methods.
`database update` applies only pending migrations, tracked in `__EFMigrationsHistory`.
Always review the generated Up() - a renamed property looks like DROP COLUMN + ADD COLUMN and destroys data.
Commit both the migration file and the updated snapshot file to keep history consistent.
`MigrateAsync()` at startup is only safe in dev or single-pod deployments.

code.note: Reserve MigrateAsync for development; in production, apply an idempotent SQL script from CI/CD before the app pods start.

answer.analogy: A migration is a version-controlled ALTER SCRIPT your team can review and roll back.
redFlags: Not reviewing the generated migration before applying - a rename produces a destructive DROP + ADD.
Calling MigrateAsync() unconditionally in all environments without thinking about pod races.
Not committing the ModelSnapshot file, which breaks the next diff.
Confusing `database update` with `migrations add` - one generates code, the other runs SQL.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF Core know which migrations are already applied?
followUpsQA.a: It reads the `__EFMigrationsHistory` table and only runs migrations whose names are not already there.

followUpsQA.q: What is the ModelSnapshot file and why must you commit it?
followUpsQA.a: It records the current model state so the next `migrations add` can diff correctly; without it the diff is wrong.

followUpsQA.q: How do you rename a column without losing data?
followUpsQA.a: Hand-edit the generated migration to use `migrationBuilder.RenameColumn` instead of the generated DROP + ADD.

followUpsQA.q: What does `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` produce?
followUpsQA.a: A SQL script with IF NOT EXISTS guards so it is safe to re-run against any database state.

followUpsQA.q: Can you apply a specific migration instead of the latest?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - `dotnet ef database update <MigrationName>` applies or rolls back to that exact point.

dialogue.text: Walk me through the migration workflow from a model change to the database.

I change the C# entity or DbContext config, then run `dotnet ef migrations add AddProductDescription`. The tooling diffs my model against the stored snapshot and generates a migration class with Up() and Down() methods. I review the file - especially for renames that look like DROP + ADD - then run `dotnet ef database update` to apply it.
How does EF know which migrations have already been applied to a database?
It checks the `__EFMigrationsHistory` table, which stores the name and EF version of each applied migration. The update command skips anything already recorded there and only runs the pending ones.
You renamed the `ProductName` property to `Title` and the migration shows DROP COLUMN then ADD COLUMN. What do you do?

That destroys the data in the column. I hand-edit the migration to replace the drop + add with `migrationBuilder.RenameColumn(name: "ProductName", table: "Products", newName: "Title")`, which maps to a safe ALTER TABLE RENAME in SQL Server.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between code-first and database-first approaches in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: Code-first defines the schema in C# and generates the database through migrations; database-first scaffolds C# entity classes and a DbContext from an existing database schema.
answer.detail: In code-first, your C# entities and `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` classes are the single source of truth. You run `dotnet ef migrations add` to capture every schema change as a versioned file, which is committed to source control. This gives you a full change history and the ability to roll back. In database-first, you point `dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold` at an existing connection string and it generates entity classes and a DbContext by reading the live schema. This is useful when a DBA owns the schema and you are building an application against it. The generated code is a starting point - it gets overwritten on the next scaffold unless you move your customizations to partial classes. You can scaffold once to bootstrap, then switch to code-first migrations going forward by adding an initial baseline migration that matches the current schema.
keyPoints: Code-first: C# model drives the schema; every change tracked via migration files in source control.
Database-first: existing schema drives the model; `dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold` generates entities.
Scaffold output is regenerated each time, so put custom code in partial classes.
You can start with database-first scaffolding then switch to code-first migrations for ongoing changes.
EF Core has no EDMX designer - code-first plus migrations is the mainstream path.

code.note: Partial classes are the safe way to extend generated database-first code without losing customizations on re-scaffold.

answer.analogy: Code-first is designing the building in blueprints; database-first is scanning an existing building to create blueprints.
redFlags: Thinking code-first creates the database without migrations - `EnsureCreated` is not migrations.
Editing generated scaffold files directly and losing changes on the next scaffold run.
Not knowing the scaffold command exists for onboarding to a legacy database.
Confusing code-first with migrations - migrations is the versioning tool for code-first, not a separate approach.

followUpsQA.q: How do you stop re-scaffold from overwriting your custom changes?
followUpsQA.a: Put customizations in partial classes or separate files that the scaffold command never touches.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use code-first migrations against an existing database?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - scaffold the entities, create an initial migration with `--ignore-changes`, then continue code-first from that point.

followUpsQA.q: Does code-first with `EnsureCreated` use migrations?
followUpsQA.a: No - `EnsureCreated` creates the schema in one go with no migration history, so you cannot use migrations later.

followUpsQA.q: Which approach is better for greenfield projects?
followUpsQA.a: Code-first with migrations - the schema history is version-controlled alongside the application code.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between code-first and database-first in EF Core?

Code-first means the C# model is the source of truth and migrations generate the database. Database-first means you point `dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold` at an existing database and it generates your entity classes and DbContext from the live schema.
Which do you default to for a new project?

Code-first - every schema change becomes a versioned migration file committed to git, so the team has a full history. Database-first is my choice when a DBA owns an existing schema and I am building an app against it.
You scaffolded the existing database, but now you want to start evolving the schema in code. How?

After scaffolding I add an initial migration with `--ignore-changes` so it matches the current database state without generating any SQL. From that point I add normal code-first migrations for future changes.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you seed data in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: Use `HasData()` in `OnModelCreating` to bake static lookup data into migrations, or write a custom idempotent seeder for large or environment-specific data.
answer.detail: HasData() is the built-in way to seed reference data. When EF detects a change in the seeded values, it generates a new migration with INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements. Every row seeded via HasData must have an explicit primary key value, even for columns normally auto-generated by the database. This lets EF track changes across migrations - if you change a seed value, it generates an UPDATE migration. For data that is too large, dynamically generated, or environment-specific (like test user accounts for dev), HasData is the wrong tool. Instead, write a seeder class that runs after migrations, uses a check before inserting, and is gated on the environment. Putting HasData inside an `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` class keeps it co-located with the entity's schema configuration and scales much better than bloating `OnModelCreating`.
keyPoints: `HasData(...)` bakes INSERTs into the migration; changes to seed values generate new migrations.
Every seeded row must supply an explicit primary key value - even for auto-generated keys.
Use a custom check-then-insert seeder for large datasets or environment-specific dev data.
Gate dev-only seeds with `IWebHostEnvironment.IsDevelopment()` to prevent them reaching production.
Place `HasData` inside `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` to keep seed data with the entity config.

code.note: HasData rows must include the PK value so EF can generate correct INSERT/UPDATE migrations when seed data changes.

answer.analogy: HasData is a migration for your rows, not just your columns.
redFlags: Not knowing HasData requires explicit primary key values - it throws at migration time without them.
Using HasData for thousands of rows or dynamic data that changes per environment.
Seeding dev test accounts via HasData, causing them to appear in production migrations.
Confusing `HasData` timing - it runs at migration time, not at app startup.

followUpsQA.q: If you change a value in HasData, does EF generate a new migration?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - HasData changes are model changes and the next `migrations add` generates an UPDATE statement for the changed row.

followUpsQA.q: How do you seed data with auto-generated primary keys?
followUpsQA.a: HasData requires you to supply explicit key values even for auto-generated columns; for truly dynamic IDs use a runtime seeder instead.

followUpsQA.q: Where is the best place to put HasData calls?
followUpsQA.a: Inside `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` so seed data is co-located with the entity's schema configuration.

followUpsQA.q: How do you write an idempotent runtime seeder?
followUpsQA.a: Check if the data already exists before inserting - for example `if (!ctx.Categories.Any()) ctx.Categories.AddRange(...)` then SaveChanges.

dialogue.text: How do you seed data in EF Core?

For static reference data like country codes or product categories, I use `HasData` inside an `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` class. That bakes the INSERTs into the migration so they are version-controlled. For large or environment-specific data, I write a custom seeder that runs after migrations and does a check before inserting.
Any gotcha with HasData you have seen trip people up?

The most common one is not supplying an explicit primary key value. Even if the column is normally auto-generated by the database, HasData requires you to set the Id manually - otherwise it throws when generating the migration.
You need different seed data for dev versus production. How do you handle that?

I skip HasData entirely for that data and use a runtime seeder gated on `IWebHostEnvironment.IsDevelopment()`. It checks whether the data already exists before inserting, so it is idempotent and only adds dev accounts or test records in the development environment.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between Fluent API and Data Annotations in EF Core, and which should you prefer?

answer.oneLiner: Fluent API in `OnModelCreating` gives complete configuration power and keeps entities clean; Data Annotations are attribute shortcuts that cover only a subset of configuration options.
answer.detail: Data Annotations like `[Required]`, `[MaxLength]`, and `[Key]` live directly on the entity class as attributes. They are quick for simple cases but they pull EF and System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations namespaces into your domain model, coupling it to the persistence framework. They also cannot express composite keys, value converters, shadow properties, owned types, or query filters. Fluent API in `OnModelCreating` can do everything annotations can do plus all of the above, and it keeps the domain entity clean with no EF attributes. The best practice is to use `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` classes - one per entity - and call `modelBuilder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly(typeof(AppDbContext).Assembly)` in `OnModelCreating` so they are auto-discovered. Fluent API wins when they conflict with annotations.
keyPoints: Data Annotations work for simple cases but cannot express composite keys, owned types, value converters, or shadow properties.
Annotations couple your domain entity to EF and validation namespaces.
Fluent API covers all configuration options and keeps entities attribute-free.
`IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` classes - one per entity - keep config organized and testable.
`ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly` auto-registers all configuration classes; no manual registration needed.

code.note: Composite keys require Fluent API - Data Annotations cannot express them at all.

answer.analogy: Annotations are sticky notes on your entity; Fluent API is a dedicated configuration file that scales.
redFlags: Trying to configure a composite key with `[Key]` attributes - it does not work.
Not knowing `ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly` exists and manually registering every entity.
Putting all Fluent API calls directly in `OnModelCreating` - it becomes unmaintainable fast.
Confusing Data Annotation `[Required]` with database-level NOT NULL configuration - they overlap but are not identical.

followUpsQA.q: What configuration can only be done via Fluent API and not annotations?
followUpsQA.a: Composite keys, value converters, owned types, shadow properties, global query filters, and table splitting.

followUpsQA.q: When Fluent API and a Data Annotation configure the same property differently, which wins?
followUpsQA.a: Fluent API always wins over Data Annotations when they conflict.

followUpsQA.q: How do you auto-register all IEntityTypeConfiguration classes?
followUpsQA.a: Call `modelBuilder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly(typeof(AppDbContext).Assembly)` in `OnModelCreating`.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use both Fluent API and annotations on the same entity?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - they can coexist; Fluent API takes priority where they conflict, so you can use annotations for simple things and Fluent API for advanced config.

dialogue.text: Fluent API versus Data Annotations - what is the practical difference?

Annotations are attributes on the entity class - quick but limited and they pull EF namespaces into your domain model. Fluent API configures everything in `OnModelCreating` and keeps the entity attribute-free, which is cleaner for a domain model.
Which do you prefer and for what reason?

Fluent API in `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` classes - one per entity. Annotations cannot express composite keys, owned types, or value converters, so Fluent API is the only option for those. It also scales better when the model grows.
Your OnModelCreating method is 500 lines long. How do you fix that?

Extract each entity's config into its own `IEntityTypeConfiguration<T>` class and replace all that code with one line: `modelBuilder.ApplyConfigurationsFromAssembly(typeof(AppDbContext).Assembly)`. It auto-discovers and registers every configuration class in the assembly.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you configure one-to-many and many-to-many relationships in EF Core using Fluent API?

answer.oneLiner: Use `HasMany().WithOne()` for one-to-many and `HasMany().WithMany()` for many-to-many; EF Core 5 and later auto-creates the join table, or you supply an explicit join entity when you need extra columns on the join.
answer.detail: For one-to-many, the common pattern is `HasMany(parent => parent.Children).WithOne(child => child.Parent).HasForeignKey(child => child.ParentId)`. Always set `OnDelete` explicitly because SQL Server rejects multiple cascade paths to the same table. For many-to-many, EF Core 5 introduced implicit configuration via `HasMany().WithMany()` - EF generates a shadow join table named by combining the two entity names. If the join needs payload columns (like the date a user was assigned to a team, or the quantity on an order line), you define an explicit join entity with a composite primary key using `HasKey(e => new { e.PostId, e.TagId })`. Composite keys must be configured via Fluent API because Data Annotations cannot express them.
keyPoints: One-to-many: `HasMany().WithOne().HasForeignKey().OnDelete(...)` - always set the delete behavior explicitly.
Many-to-many implicit (EF Core 5+): `HasMany().WithMany()` auto-generates a hidden join table.
Many-to-many with payload columns: define an explicit join entity with a composite primary key.
SQL Server rejects multiple cascade paths to one table - use `DeleteBehavior.Restrict` to break the cycle.
Composite keys on join entities must use Fluent API `HasKey` - annotations cannot express them.

code.note: An explicit join entity with `HasKey(e => new { ... })` is required when the join table needs payload columns like `EnrolledAt`.

answer.analogy: A join entity with payload is a shipping manifest - not just who connected to whom, but when and how much.
redFlags: Trying to configure a composite key with `[Key]` annotations alone - it does not work.
Forgetting to set `OnDelete` explicitly and hitting SQL Server cascade cycle errors at migration time.
Not knowing EF Core 5 supports implicit many-to-many without a join entity class.
Adding columns to the implicit join table instead of switching to an explicit join entity.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF name the auto-generated join table for implicit many-to-many?
followUpsQA.a: By convention it combines both entity names alphabetically, for example `CourseStudent` for Course and Student.

followUpsQA.q: When do you need an explicit join entity instead of implicit many-to-many?
followUpsQA.a: When the join table needs payload columns like `EnrolledAt`, `Quantity`, or `AssignedBy`.

followUpsQA.q: How do you fix a SQL Server cascade cycle rejection?
followUpsQA.a: Set `OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Restrict)` or `NoAction` on one of the relationships causing the cycle, and handle the cascaded deletes in application code.

followUpsQA.q: How do you configure a self-referencing hierarchy like a product category tree?
followUpsQA.a: Use `HasOne(c => c.Parent).WithMany(c => c.Children).HasForeignKey(c => c.ParentId)` with a nullable FK.

dialogue.text: How do you configure a one-to-many relationship with Fluent API?

`builder.HasMany(o => o.Items).WithOne(i => i.Order).HasForeignKey(i => i.OrderId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade)`. I always set OnDelete explicitly because SQL Server will reject a migration if there are multiple cascade paths to the same table.
And many-to-many - how does that work in modern EF Core?

Since EF Core 5 you can just write `HasMany(s => s.Courses).WithMany(c => c.Students)` and EF auto-generates the join table. If the join needs extra columns - like an enrollment date or a grade - I define an explicit join entity with a composite key using `HasKey(e => new { e.StudentId, e.CourseId })`.
SQL Server rejected your migration with a multiple cascade paths error. What do you do?

That happens when two relationships both cascade to the same table, which SQL Server does not allow. I set `OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Restrict)` on one of them to break the cycle, and handle the cleanup manually in a service method.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you handle EF Core migrations safely in a production environment?

answer.oneLiner: Generate an idempotent SQL script and apply it as a dedicated step in CI/CD before the app deploys, so a DBA can review it, it runs once, and you can roll back if needed.
answer.detail: In production, you should never call `MigrateAsync()` at application startup because multiple pod replicas will race each other to apply the same migrations. Instead, generate an idempotent migration script with `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent`. This produces SQL with IF NOT EXISTS guards around each change, so re-running it is safe. Apply it in a CI/CD pipeline stage or a Kubernetes init-container that runs before the main app pods start. This also allows a DBA to review and sign off on the SQL before it touches the production database. For zero-downtime blue-green deployments, schema changes must be backward compatible with the old version of the app still running. This means you can safely add nullable columns or new tables, but renaming or dropping columns breaks the old app. Use the expand-contract pattern: add the new column and dual-write to both, migrate data, switch reads to the new column, then drop the old column in a later release.
keyPoints: `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` generates SQL with IF NOT EXISTS guards safe to re-run.
Apply migrations as a separate CI/CD step or init-container before app pods start - never at startup.
Multiple pods calling `MigrateAsync()` simultaneously race and can corrupt the migration history.
In blue-green deployments, only add backward-compatible changes: nullable columns, new tables.
Renames and drops break the old app version still running - use expand-contract over two releases.

code.note: Reserve `MigrateAsync()` for development; in production, apply the idempotent SQL script before the app starts.

answer.analogy: Migrations in production are like a database surgery - you plan, review, and execute before the patient wakes up.
redFlags: Calling `MigrateAsync()` unconditionally in all environments - racing pods cause silent corruption.
Applying breaking changes like column drops in a zero-downtime deployment without expand-contract.
Not generating an idempotent script, so re-running the SQL on failure causes errors.
Skipping DBA review on production schema changes - indexes and constraints matter for performance.

followUpsQA.q: What is the expand-contract pattern and why does zero-downtime need it?
followUpsQA.a: Add the new column (expand), migrate data and dual-write, switch reads to the new column, then in a later release remove the old one (contract) - so both app versions work at the same time.

followUpsQA.q: How do you roll back a failed migration in production?
followUpsQA.a: Run `dotnet ef database update <PreviousMigrationName>` which applies the Down() methods, or run a pre-generated rollback script.

followUpsQA.q: Why use idempotent scripts instead of a plain script?
followUpsQA.a: Idempotent scripts include IF NOT EXISTS guards so re-running after a partial failure is safe.

followUpsQA.q: What schema changes are safe during a blue-green zero-downtime deployment?
followUpsQA.a: Adding nullable columns, adding new tables, and adding indexes - anything the old app version ignores.

dialogue.text: How do you handle EF Core migrations safely in a production environment?

I never call `MigrateAsync()` at app startup in production. Instead I generate an idempotent SQL script with `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` and apply it in a dedicated CI/CD stage - or a Kubernetes init-container - before the app pods start. That runs the migration exactly once and lets a DBA review it first.
Why does MigrateAsync at startup cause problems with multiple pods?

When three pods start simultaneously, all three query `__EFMigrationsHistory`, see pending migrations, and try to apply them at the same time. They race to write to the history table and can corrupt it or cause constraint violations. A separate apply step runs once before any pod starts.
You are doing a zero-downtime blue-green deployment. Which schema changes are safe to apply while the old app is still running?

Adding nullable columns or new tables - the old app ignores them. Renaming or dropping columns breaks the old app immediately because its queries still reference the old name. For those I use expand-contract: add the new column first, dual-write to both versions, then drop the old one in the next release.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How does optimistic concurrency work in EF Core and how do you configure a concurrency token?

answer.oneLiner: EF adds the token's original value to the UPDATE WHERE clause; if another process changed the row, zero rows are affected and EF throws `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`.
answer.detail: Optimistic concurrency assumes conflicts are rare - it does not lock the row during reading. Instead, it adds a token (a version stamp) to the WHERE clause of every UPDATE or DELETE. The SQL becomes `UPDATE Orders SET Status = 'Shipped' WHERE Id = 42 AND RowVersion = 0x000000001234`. If another user already updated the row, the RowVersion changed, zero rows match the WHERE, and EF counts zero rows affected. When it expects at least one, it throws `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`. You configure the token with `.IsRowVersion()` for a SQL Server `rowversion` or `timestamp` column, which the database auto-increments on every update. Alternatively use `[ConcurrencyCheck]` or `.IsConcurrencyToken()` on a specific property like `UpdatedAt`. In a REST API, expose the RowVersion as an ETag in the response header and require the client to send `If-Match` on updates. Set the `OriginalValue` from the client's version so EF uses it in the WHERE clause.
keyPoints: The token's original value is added to the UPDATE/DELETE WHERE clause.
Zero rows affected means a conflict occurred; EF throws `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`.
SQL Server `rowversion` with `.IsRowVersion()` is the most reliable token - the database auto-updates it.
In a REST API, expose RowVersion as an ETag header and require `If-Match` on updates.
Set `entry.Property(r => r.RowVersion).OriginalValue` to the client-provided version so EF uses it in the WHERE clause.

code.note: Setting `OriginalValue` on the RowVersion ensures EF uses the client-provided version in the WHERE clause, not the version it loaded from the database.

answer.analogy: Optimistic concurrency is like submitting a form with a version stamp - if someone else submitted first, your version is stale and the server rejects you.
redFlags: Confusing optimistic concurrency with pessimistic locking - they are different strategies.
Not handling `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`, letting it bubble up as an unhandled 500 error.
Not setting `OriginalValue` on RowVersion, causing EF to use the wrong version in the WHERE clause.
Using a manual timestamp token instead of `rowversion` - timestamps can collide under load.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency?
followUpsQA.a: Optimistic detects conflicts at save time with a token; pessimistic locks the row during reading with `SELECT FOR UPDATE` or `UPDLOCK` to prevent concurrent access.

followUpsQA.q: How do you implement ETags with EF Core RowVersion?
followUpsQA.a: Convert the `byte[]` RowVersion to a base64 string, return it as an `ETag` response header, and require the client to send `If-Match` on updates; reject mismatches with 409 Conflict.

followUpsQA.q: What exception signals a concurrency conflict?
followUpsQA.a: `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`, thrown when EF detects zero rows were affected by an UPDATE or DELETE.

followUpsQA.q: How do you resolve a concurrency conflict in the exception handler?
followUpsQA.a: Call `entry.ReloadAsync()` to get the latest values, then re-apply the user's changes on top and retry, or return a 409 with the current state so the client can merge.

dialogue.text: How does optimistic concurrency work in EF Core?

EF adds the concurrency token's original value to the WHERE clause of the UPDATE or DELETE. So instead of `WHERE Id = 42`, it becomes `WHERE Id = 42 AND RowVersion = 0x0001`. If another process changed the row first, that RowVersion no longer matches, zero rows are affected, and EF throws `DbUpdateConcurrencyException`.
How do you configure the concurrency token?

For SQL Server I use a `rowversion` column mapped with `.IsRowVersion()`. The database auto-increments it on every update, so it is always accurate. Alternatively `[ConcurrencyCheck]` or `.IsConcurrencyToken()` works on any property, but a manual timestamp can collide under load.
You are building a REST API. How does the client send the version it expects?

I expose the `byte[]` RowVersion as a base64-encoded `ETag` header in the GET response. On the PUT request, the client sends `If-Match` with that value. I decode it, set `entry.Property(r => r.RowVersion).OriginalValue` to the decoded bytes, and EF uses it in the WHERE clause.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What goes inside a migration's Up() and Down() methods, and what can you do beyond basic schema changes?

answer.oneLiner: Up() applies the change and Down() reverses it using `MigrationBuilder` methods for schema, plus `migrationBuilder.Sql()` for raw SQL to backfill data, create views, or define stored procedures.
answer.detail: EF generates standard schema operations like `AddColumn`, `CreateTable`, `CreateIndex`, and `AddForeignKey` in Up() and their reverses in Down(). Beyond schema, you can call `migrationBuilder.Sql("...")` to run any arbitrary SQL inside the same migration transaction. This is essential for data migrations - for example, adding a non-nullable column to a populated table requires you to add it as nullable first, backfill existing rows with SQL, then alter it to NOT NULL, all in one migration to keep the deployment atomic. You can also use `Sql()` to create database views, stored procedures, or functions that EF does not track natively. Because EF does not track objects created via raw SQL, you must manually drop them in Down(). For statements that cannot run inside a transaction - such as certain online index operations - pass `suppressTransaction: true` to the `Sql()` call. Always keep Down() accurate so rollback restores the prior schema correctly.
keyPoints: `MigrationBuilder` methods cover tables, columns, indexes, and foreign keys for standard schema ops.
`migrationBuilder.Sql("...")` runs raw SQL for data backfills, views, or stored procedures.
Down() must exactly reverse Up() - an empty Down() makes rollback impossible.
EF does not track views or stored procs created via Sql(), so you must drop them manually in Down().
`suppressTransaction: true` on Sql() is for operations that cannot run inside a transaction.

code.note: Combining schema change and data backfill in one migration keeps the deployment atomic - the column is never in an inconsistent state between steps.

answer.analogy: Up is the surgery; Down is the patient notes telling the next doctor how to undo it.
redFlags: Leaving Down() empty - rolling back a migration with no Down() is impossible without manual SQL.
Adding a NOT NULL column to a populated table without a backfill step - the database rejects it.
Not knowing you can run raw SQL inside a migration for data backfills or DDL objects like views.
Creating a view via Sql() in Up() but forgetting to drop it in Down(), leaving the schema inconsistent on rollback.

followUpsQA.q: How do you create a database view via EF Core migrations?
followUpsQA.a: Use `migrationBuilder.Sql("CREATE VIEW vw_ActiveOrders AS ...")` in Up() and `migrationBuilder.Sql("DROP VIEW vw_ActiveOrders")` in Down().

followUpsQA.q: When do you need `suppressTransaction: true` on a Sql() call?
followUpsQA.a: For DDL statements that cannot run inside a transaction, such as certain online index rebuilds or database-level configuration changes.

followUpsQA.q: Why must Down() exactly reverse Up()?
followUpsQA.a: So `dotnet ef database update <PreviousName>` restores the database to a known clean state, enabling reliable rollback.

followUpsQA.q: Does EF Core track views and stored procedures created via Sql() in migrations?
followUpsQA.a: No - EF only tracks entities defined in the model; anything created via raw Sql() must be manually dropped in Down().

dialogue.text: What goes inside a migration's Up and Down methods?

Up applies the schema change using `MigrationBuilder` methods - AddColumn, CreateTable, CreateIndex, AddForeignKey. Down reverses each of those operations. You can also call `migrationBuilder.Sql()` to run raw SQL for anything MigrationBuilder does not cover natively.
Can you give a real example of when you would use Sql() inside a migration?

Adding a NOT NULL boolean column to a Customers table that already has rows. I add it as nullable first, run `Sql("UPDATE Customers SET IsVerified = 0")` to backfill all existing rows, then alter it to NOT NULL. Everything in one atomic migration so the column is never in a broken state.
You create a reporting view via Sql() in Up. What must you do in Down?
Manually drop it - EF does not track objects created by raw SQL. If I forget the DROP VIEW in Down, rolling back leaves the view in the database and the schema is inconsistent.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you configure owned entity types and value objects in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: Use `OwnsOne` or `OwnsMany` to map DDD value objects - EF stores their columns in the owner's table by default, with no independent primary key or DbSet.
answer.detail: A value object in domain-driven design has no identity of its own - it is defined by its values (an Address is just a combination of Street, City, and PostCode). EF Core's owned entity mechanism maps this pattern directly. `OwnsOne(o => o.ShippingAddress)` stores the address columns directly in the owner's table, prefixed by convention (e.g., `ShippingAddress_Street`, `ShippingAddress_City`). You can rename them with `.HasColumnName()`. `OwnsMany(o => o.LineItems)` stores a collection of value objects in a separate table with a FK to the owner but no independent primary key. Owned entities have no DbSet and cannot be queried independently of the owner. They always load with the owner - there is no lazy loading. To store a single value object in its own table, use `OwnsOne` combined with `.ToTable("Addresses")`. If all owned properties are null in the database, the owned reference comes back as null.
keyPoints: `OwnsOne` maps a single-value object as columns in the owner's table - no separate primary key.
`OwnsMany` maps a collection of value objects to a separate table with an owner FK but no own key.
Owned entities have no DbSet - you cannot query them independently.
They always load with the owner - no lazy loading applies.
Use `ToTable()` inside `OwnsOne` or `OwnsMany` to store the value object in its own table.

code.note: `OwnsMany` creates a separate table with a FK to the owner and no own primary key - the items cannot exist without the order.

answer.analogy: An owned entity is a part of the owner, not a separate citizen with its own passport.
redFlags: Trying to query owned entities via `DbSet<Address>` - they have no DbSet and this throws.
Confusing owned entities with regular related entities - owned types have no independent identity.
Expecting lazy loading to work for owned properties - they always load with the owner.
Not knowing `OwnsOne` stores columns in the parent table by default, not a separate table.

followUpsQA.q: Can you query owned types independently using a DbSet?
followUpsQA.a: No - owned types have no DbSet and can only be accessed through the owner entity.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF handle a null owned entity when reading from the database?
followUpsQA.a: If all owned property columns are null, EF returns null for the owned reference - for example, `order.ShippingAddress` is null if all its columns are null.

followUpsQA.q: How do you store an owned value object in its own table instead of the owner's table?
followUpsQA.a: Call `.ToTable("Addresses")` inside the `OwnsOne` or `OwnsMany` configuration lambda.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between an owned entity and table splitting?
followUpsQA.a: Table splitting maps two separate entity types to the same table, both with independent keys; owned types are value objects with no key of their own stored as columns of the owner.

dialogue.text: How do you configure owned entity types in EF Core?

With `OwnsOne` or `OwnsMany` in the entity's `IEntityTypeConfiguration`. By default, `OwnsOne` stores the value object's columns directly in the owner's table - so an `Order` with a `ShippingAddress` gets columns like `ShippingAddress_Street` and `ShippingAddress_City` in the Orders table with no separate table or key.
And for a collection of value objects, like order line items?

`OwnsMany` creates a separate table with a FK back to the owner but no independent primary key. The line items cannot exist without the order - that is the key semantic difference from a regular one-to-many relationship.
A junior developer tries to write `ctx.ShippingAddresses.Where(...)`. What happens?

It fails at compile time - owned types have no DbSet. They are not independent citizens in the model. You can only reach them through their owner: `ctx.Orders.Include(o => o.ShippingAddress)` or just loading the order and accessing the property.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
